text:
EXAMINATION: Gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT, whole body with diagnostic CT

CLINICAL INDICATION: 70-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Initially diagnosed with stage IV (T3b N1 M1b) prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), with bone and nodal metastases. Treated with ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone for approximately 24 months with initial biochemical and radiographic response. Now with PSA rising from nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 4.7 ng/mL over three consecutive measurements in the setting of castrate testosterone levels, fulfilling criteria for castration-resistant progression. Known germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant. Study performed for restaging and disease characterization prior to next-line therapy.

TECHNIQUE: Intravenous administration of Ga-68 PSMA-11 (approximately 185 MBq). Whole-body PET imaging was obtained approximately 60 minutes post-injection from the vertex of the skull to mid-thighs, with supplemental imaging through the pelvis and proximal femora. Co-registered low-dose CT was performed for attenuation correction and anatomical localization. A separately acquired diagnostic CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast was obtained in the same session. PET image quality is good. No adverse reaction to the radiopharmaceutical or contrast agent.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No PSMA-avid intracranial or calvarial lesions. No PSMA-avid cervical lymphadenopathy. The parotid and submandibular glands demonstrate expected physiologic PSMA uptake bilaterally. Thyroid gland unremarkable.

CHEST:
Lungs: No focal pulmonary nodules or masses on low-dose CT. No pleural effusions. The mediastinal and hilar structures are unremarkable. No PSMA-avid pulmonary or pleural lesion.

Mediastinum and Lymph Nodes: No PSMA-avid mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes by CT size criteria.

Sternum and Ribs: There is a new focus of moderately intense PSMA avidity involving the right posterior 7th rib at the costovertebral junction. CT shows subtle sclerotic density at this site without a discrete lytic component. A second new focus of PSMA avidity is identified at the left posterior 9th rib, again with subtle underlying sclerotic change on CT. These foci were not identified on the prior conventional bone scan approximately 12 months earlier and are consistent with new osseous metastatic deposits.

THORACIC SPINE:
At the T10 vertebral body, there is a new focus of moderately intense PSMA uptake (SUV max approximately 6.4). Corresponding CT demonstrates patchy sclerotic density involving the mid-vertebral body without significant cortical disruption or epidural extension. This was not present on prior imaging and represents a new osseous metastasis. No other PSMA-avid thoracic vertebral lesions are identified. No pathological fracture. No epidural or paraspinal soft tissue component.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: No PSMA-avid hepatic lesions. The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic abnormality on contrast-enhanced CT.

Spleen, Pancreas, Adrenal Glands, Kidneys: Unremarkable. The adrenal glands show no PSMA-avid lesions. No adrenal enlargement. Physiologic renal excretion of PSMA tracer is noted bilaterally. No renal masses.

Retroperitoneal and Mesenteric Lymph Nodes: No PSMA-avid retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy above the aortic bifurcation. No pathological adenopathy by size criteria in this distribution.

LUMBAR SPINE:
The previously identified lesions at L3 and L4 vertebral bodies demonstrate interval increase in PSMA avidity compared to the prior restaging bone scan and CT. The L3 lesion shows heterogeneous, moderately intense tracer uptake (SUV max approximately 8.1), and the L4 lesion demonstrates similarly increased uptake (SUV max approximately 7.3). On CT, both lesions appear predominantly sclerotic but are more prominent in extent compared to prior imaging. There is no associated epidural soft tissue component and no spinal canal compromise. These findings are in keeping with progressive disease at known sites of osseous involvement. No new lumbar vertebral lesions below L4.

PELVIS:
Lymph Nodes: The right obturator lymph node, previously measuring 1.2 cm at diagnosis and 0.7 cm on restaging during treatment response, has now enlarged to 1.4 cm in short axis on CT. It demonstrates increased PSMA avidity (SUV max approximately 9.2) compared to prior imaging, confirming active nodal metastatic involvement and interval progression at this site. No other pelvic lymph nodes meet size criteria for pathological enlargement, though a small right internal iliac lymph node measuring 0.8 cm demonstrates faint PSMA uptake that is indeterminate.

Bladder: Normal. Expected physiologic PSMA tracer accumulation in the urinary bladder. Post-treatment changes in the pelvis are noted, consistent with prior prostate disease history. No residual PSMA-avid signal in the prostate bed.

Right Sacral Ala: A new, discrete focus of moderately intense PSMA uptake is identified in the right sacral ala (SUV max approximately 5.8). CT demonstrates faint sclerotic density at this site. This focus was not identified on prior bone scan and is consistent with a new osseous metastatic deposit. No cortical breakthrough. No associated soft tissue mass.

Right Iliac Wing: The previously identified lesion at the right iliac wing demonstrates continued PSMA avidity, appearing stable to minimally increased in intensity compared to prior bone scan. CT shows predominantly sclerotic changes. No new lesion in the contralateral iliac wing.

Right Femoral Neck: The previously identified lesion at the right femoral neck demonstrates persistent PSMA avidity. CT appearance is stable with sclerotic morphology. No pathological fracture or cortical breach.

LOWER EXTREMITIES:
No additional PSMA-avid foci in the visualized bilateral femora or proximal tibiae.

PSMA-NEGATIVE LESIONS:
No lesions suspicious for malignancy on CT that lack corresponding PSMA avidity are identified. No PSMA-negative visceral masses or lymph nodes of concern.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Osseous disease progression compared to prior restaging bone scan. New PSMA-avid lesions are identified at the following sites not previously documented: T10 vertebral body, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, and right sacral ala — representing at least four new bone metastases. Previously identified lesions at L3 and L4 vertebral bodies show increased PSMA avidity consistent with active progression at these sites.

2. Stable osseous metastatic foci at the right iliac wing and right femoral neck, with sclerotic morphology suggesting prior treatment effect, though these sites remain PSMA-avid.

3. Interval enlargement and increased PSMA avidity of the right obturator lymph node (now 1.4 cm, previously 0.7 cm on prior restaging CT), consistent with nodal disease progression. A small right internal iliac lymph node with faint PSMA uptake is indeterminate and warrants follow-up.

4. No visceral metastases. No hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other soft tissue metastatic deposits identified.

5. No PSMA-negative lesions to suggest dedifferentiated or neuroendocrine subclone not captured by PSMA-based imaging at this time.

Overall, the findings are consistent with progressive osseous and nodal metastatic disease in the setting of established castration-resistant prostate cancer, with multiple new bone sites and nodal enlargement. The extent of PSMA-avid disease suggests systemic progression and these findings were communicated to the treating oncology team at the time of interpretation.

Attending Radiologist: [Electronically signed]
Nuclear Medicine / Molecular Imaging
